title: The Fifth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sunday, July 14, 2019 | Preacher: Katie Pearson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Fifth Sunday after Pentecost, placing the congregation in Ordinary Time (the long green season following Pentecost). Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings would have included 2 Samuel 6:1-5, 17-19 (David bringing the ark to Jerusalem), Psalm 24, Ephesians 1:3-14 (spiritual blessings in Christ), and Mark 6:14-29 (the death of John the Baptist). The Baptist's beheading narrative, while appearing annually in this lectionary slot, often resonates with particular intensity depending on contemporary circumstances of violence or persecution.

---

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a period of relative institutional stability in mid-2019, though still navigating ongoing tensions around LGBTQ+ inclusion and biblical interpretation that had characterized the post-2018 General Convention period. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop Rob Hirschfeld (who served from 2017-2024), was focused on local mission work and diocesan development. There were no major denominational crises or bishop elections in process at this specific moment, though the broader Anglican Communion continued its fractious debates over sexuality and authority that had defined the previous several years.

---

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was in the midst of a typical hot, dry July with wildfires active across Colorado and surrounding states—the summer fire season was well underway. Locally, the Denver area was experiencing economic growth and continued gentrification pressures in Capitol Hill and surrounding neighborhoods. The week prior would have included coverage of ongoing immigration enforcement issues, as Colorado remained a state with significant undocumented populations and active ICE operations, a topic that would have been relevant to Episcopal social justice conversations.

---

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The Trump administration was actively enforcing immigration policy, with ICE raids occurring across the country in the week of July 8-14, including operations in Mississippi targeting undocumented workers. Mueller's investigation into Russian interference in the 2016 election had recently concluded (late March 2019), but Congressional testimony and its implications continued dominating political discourse. Democratic primary debates for the 2020 election were beginning, with the first official debates held July 30-31, creating significant political polarization and uncertainty about the coming election cycle.

---

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Hong Kong was experiencing the early stages of the 2019 pro-democracy protests, which began in early June over an extradition bill and would escalate significantly in the months ahead. The Israeli-Palestinian conflict remained unstable following increased tensions and military actions in Gaza. The global climate crisis was receiving increased media attention following the recent European heat waves and the continued march toward the 2019 UN climate discussions.

---

CULTURAL MOMENT

The summer of 2019 carried an anxious, divided cultural mood in the United States—politically polarized, concerned about immigration and environmental futures, yet also invested in entertainment and sports as escape. The Lion King remake had just been released (July 9), dominating cultural conversation. The Marvel Cinematic Universe remained culturally dominant. There was a pervasive sense of impending crisis (climate, political, social) combined with fatigue from ongoing national division. For a Denver congregation, outdoor recreation, environmental stewardship, and immigrant solidarity would have been culturally resonant concerns.
